text: A white-cloaked figure sits in one corner of the bar, marked by a red cross stitched over the breast. The strangest thing is the empty space around them: in Kanta's Den, where every stool and standing place is usually fought over, no one pushes close.

The din of Kanta's Den swallows most attempts at quiet conversation, but the stranger seems untouched by it. Their cloak is travel-stained, the red cross faded by vacuum-dust and sun, yet their hands are clean.

On the table beside them sits a small kit of field dressings, sealant foam, and old-fashioned prayer beads worn almost smooth.

"Walk with Ludd, captain," the Hospitaller says. "Sit, if you mean no harm."
options:
koh_lore_who:"Who are you, and why are you here?"
koh_lore_kanta:"Kanta lets you sit here untouched?"
koh_lore_leave:Leave

text:
"We are the Knights Hospitaller," the stranger says. "Not conquerors. Not judges. Not priests, though some of us pray more than is useful."

A bottle breaks somewhere behind you. Nobody at the table flinches.

"A knight goes where there is suffering," they continue, "and tries to make less of it. Pirates bleed. Pathers bleed. Hegemony inspectors bleed. The dying have fewer politics than the living."

For the first time, the Hospitaller smiles.

"Kanta is not sentimental. She is practical. Men and women who expect to be shot prefer knowing someone nearby can close a wound."

They glance over the room, and more than one hardened pirate looks away.

"There is an understanding. We do not preach in her hall. Her people do not interfere when stretchers are needed."

Before you can ask another question, a shout rises by the bar. A deckhand collapses, one hand clamped over a wound darkening their shirt.

The Hospitaller is already standing.

"Forgive me, captain. Mercy rarely waits for ceremony."

They cross the room, and the crowd parts without a word.

As they go, they nod once toward you. "If you would know us better, look to Gilead. The Mercy keeps watch there."

The Invictus answers with a plain white crest marked by a red cross.

After a moment, the commander appears. Their armor is austere, the kind worn under a cloak rather than over a parade ground. Behind them, the bridge is quiet but busy: medicae stations, shuttle traffic plots, casualty manifests.

"This is the Mercy of Gilead," the commander says. "Speak, captain."

options: koh_lore_mercy_deeds:"There are Luddic matters you may know me by."
koh_lore_mercy_grant:"I cast the Planetkiller into a black hole."

text:
The commander studies you for a quiet moment, then nods to an aide off-screen.

"We keep our own accounts," they say. "Not all of them are written in ledgers."
"Then perhaps your road has not yet crossed ours in full," the commander says. "Mercy is patient. Faith, less so."
"You walked the Pilgrim's Path," the commander says. "Or near enough that the shrines remember your name."
"The Knights Errant are not quiet about those who aid them," the commander says. "You have been seen."
"False Idols leaves a deep wound," the commander says. "Some wounds close. Some teach."
"Gideon Oak's people recorded your surrender of the Planetkiller," the commander says. "A grave deed, and not without merit."

Your $shipOrFleet holds station at the edge of the abyss.

The Planetkiller is secured in your flagship under more locks, prayers, threat protocols, and armed watches than any object should require. Every faction in the Sector would kill for it. Every faction in the Sector would claim they had no choice.

The black hole waits without opinion.

koh_lore_pk_black_hole_dispose:Cast the Planetkiller into the black hole
